title: Simulation of additive binding energies in asphalt using quantum-selected configuration interaction (QSCI)
abstract: Quantum-centric supercomputing (in which a quantum processor samples the dominant electronic configurations and classical high-performance computing resources perform the diagonalisation) is emerging as a practical route to correlated electronic-structure calculations. We present QuantumPave, a hybrid quantum-classical workflow for computing additive binding energies in asphalt binder, a quantity central to the oxidative ageing of road infrastructure. Using a 24-atom pyridine-phenol hydrogen-bonded complex as a representative model, we couple machine-learning interatomic potentials (ORB v3) for geometry optimisation with quantum-selected configuration interaction (QSCI), also referred to as sample based quantum diagonalisation (SQD), in a (10e, 10o) active space run on the 54-qubit IQM Emerald processor. On hardware, SQD reproduces the active-space CASCI reference exactly, giving a binding energy of -3.52 kcal/mol (-0.153 eV); the device noise broadens the sampling to span the active space, so no zero-noise extrapolation is required. This active-space value describes a physical hydrogen bond that underbinds the calorimetric enthalpy of about -6.25 kcal/mol, the difference reflecting the compact active space. We show that chemically meaningful binding energies for an industrially relevant materials problem are attainable on current quantum hardware within a quantum-centric supercomputing workflow.
summary: This paper demonstrates a hybrid quantum-classical workflow called QuantumPave that uses a quantum processor to sample electronic configurations for calculating binding energies. By simulating a pyridine-phenol complex, the authors show that current NISQ-era hardware can achieve chemically relevant results for industrial materials science problems. The approach highlights the potential of quantum-centric supercomputing to solve complex molecular recognition tasks.
main_problem: The study aims to demonstrate the feasibility of using a quantum-centric supercomputing workflow to predict additive binding energies in asphalt, a critical factor in road infrastructure durability.
main_result: The QSCI/SQD algorithm on the 54-qubit IQM Emerald processor exactly reproduced the active-space CASCI reference binding energy of -3.52 kcal/mol, proving that chemically meaningful results are attainable on current NISQ hardware.
method: A hybrid quantum-classical workflow (QuantumPave) using Quantum-Selected Configuration Interaction (QSCI) where a quantum processor samples electronic configurations and classical HPC performs diagonalization, integrated with machine-learning interatomic potentials (ORB v3) for geometry optimization.
model_or_system: A 24-atom pyridine-phenol hydrogen-bonded complex (C11H11NO) serving as a representative model for asphalt binder interactions, mapped onto a 54-qubit IQM Emerald superconducting processor using a (10e, 10o) active space.
key_observables: Additive binding energy (in kcal/mol and eV).
important_parameters: Active space of 10 electrons in 10 orbitals; 5000 measurement shots; 10^-6 Ha energy tolerance; NISQ regime.
main_assumptions: The discrepancy between calculated and experimental values is attributed to the use of a compact/limited active space; the study assumes the pyridine-phenol complex is a sufficient proxy for asphalt binder interactions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the molecular structure of the pyridine-phenol complex; Figure 2 displays the 3D optimized structure from ORB v3; Figure 3 illustrates the QSCI workflow loop.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the industrial problem of asphalt aging, presents the QuantumPave hybrid workflow, details the QSCI algorithm and hardware implementation, compares results against classical (CASCI, DFT) and experimental benchmarks, and concludes with future scaling directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Molecular structure of the pyridine-phenol complex. The system consists of 24 atoms (C11H11NO) and is held together by an O-H· · · N hydrogen bond between the phenol hydroxyl group and the pyridine nitrogen.

Fig. 2. Three-dimensional visualisation of the optimised pyridine-phenol complex structure obtained from ORB v3 calculations (Energy: -148.936 eV, 103 optimisation steps). The structure shows the hydrogen bonding interaction between the nitrogen in pyridine and oxygen in phenol, with molecular dimensions of 5.5 × 6.3 × 4.1 ˚A.
Fig. 3. QSCI workflow showing the active space selection, configuration sampling, and energy convergence procedures.
